ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (Beef meat, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific tagliata di manzo entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for tagliata di manzo or a sufficiently close prepared beef dish concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of tagliata di manzo is approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 200 g of sliced grilled ribeye beef, 100 g of mashed potatoes, 80 g of seasonal vegetables, and 20 g of beef jus and Parmesan flakes.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, ribeye steak, boneless, lip-on, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, cooked, grilled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #23376; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entries for mashed potatoes (SR Legacy #11371) and Parmesan cheese (SR Legacy #01032) for composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Tagliata di Manzo is a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for grilled ribeye beef (dominant component, ~50% by weight), mashed potatoes (~25%), seasonal vegetables (~20%), and Parmesan flakes and beef jus (~5%). A weighted composite per-100g profile was calculated. This is a medium-confidence estimate; exact restaurant recipe not available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; GI of mashed potato (boiled, mashed) referenced as approximately 83–87; ribeye beef GI effectively 0 (protein/fat food); composite dish GI estimated at ~45 weighted by carbohydrate contribution from mashed potato component
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Tagliata di Manzo has no direct GI measurement in the University of Sydney GI Database. The composite GI is estimated as follows: beef ribeye contributes negligible glycemic carbohydrate (GI ~0 for pure protein/fat foods); mashed potato is the primary carbohydrate source (GI ~85 per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables); seasonal vegetables contribute low-GI carbohydrate (~GI 15). Weighted by carbohydrate content per serving (mashed potato ~20g carb, vegetables ~5g carb, total ~25g), the composite GI is estimated at approximately 45, placing it in the low category (≤55). GL per serving = (45 × 34g total carbohydrate per 400g serving) / 100 = 15.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from beef jus, Parmesan, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g (840 mg per standard 400 g serving), this dish contributes a substantial portion of the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced seasoning and limit beef jus quantity.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Mashed potato component with moderate-to-high GI carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: The mashed potato component contributes approximately 34 g of carbohydrate per serving with a composite dish GI of ~45 (low category), yielding a glycemic load of ~15 per serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size of the potato component and pair with the high-protein beef to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ribeye beef and Parmesan, and dietary cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ribeye beef and Parmesan flakes contribute saturated fat (~3.1 g/100 g, ~12.4 g per serving) and cholesterol (~52 mg/100 g, ~208 mg per serving); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and consider requesting leaner cuts or reduced Parmesan to align with AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tagliata di Manzo is a classic Italian grilled beef dish whose name literally means 'sliced beef' in Italian. It is prepared by grilling a thick cut of high-quality beef — traditionally ribeye or sirloin — to medium-rare, then resting the meat and slicing it thinly on the diagonal before serving. In this Singapore restaurant version served at Ricciotti - The Riverwalk, the dish features Argentine beef ribeye as the centrepiece, accompanied by a rich beef jus, shaved Parmesan flakes, creamy mashed potatoes, and seasonal vegetables, and is offered as a gluten-free option. The beef is characteristically tender, juicy, and deeply savoury, with the Parmesan adding a sharp, salty umami note and the jus providing richness. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-dense (approximately 14.8 g protein per 100 g) and moderate in fat (8.2 g/100 g), with saturated fat primarily from the ribeye and Parmesan. The carbohydrate content (~8.5 g/100 g) derives mainly from the mashed potato component. The composite glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low GI), though the mashed potato portion has a higher individual GI. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium from seasoning and Parmesan, moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the mashed potato carbohydrate load, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat and cholesterol from ribeye and Parmesan. It is naturally gluten-free as served, making it suitable for coeliac diners. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding dairy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tagliata di manzo?
What does tagliata mean in Italian?
Is tagliata di manzo good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact match for tagliata di manzo as a composite dish exists in any approved primary nutrient database. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for individual components (grilled ribeye beef, mashed potatoes, seasonal vegetables, Parmesan) weighted by approximate serving proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on the carbohydrate-contributing components. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input 'Grains and staples' to 'Meat and alternatives' — the dish is beef-dominant and the input classification was incorrect. 2. The (GF) suffix in the input food_name indicates gluten-free; this is reflected in dietary_flags.is_gluten_free = true and retained in aliases. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this composite dish in USDA FoodData Central component sources. 4. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this composite dish in USDA FoodData Central component sources. 5. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and selenium are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and carry medium confidence. 6. This dish is Italian cuisine, not Singaporean; therefore mandatory multilingual fields (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not required under G6/Q8 rules — only Italian/English naming applies. 7. GI is an analogous composite estimate; no direct GI measurement exists for tagliata di manzo in the University of Sydney GI Database. 8. Sodium estimate includes contribution from beef jus, Parmesan, and typical seasoning; actual restaurant sodium may vary. 9. Serving size of 400 g is an estimate based on typical Italian restaurant main course portions for a beef dish of this type.
